content:
# 2 Corinthians Chapter 5 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2CO_005_page_001.jpg

1. For we know that if the earthly house of the tent of us is destroyed, we have a building from God, a house not made with hands, eternal in the heavens.

2. For indeed in this we groan, longing to put on our dwelling from heaven,

3. if indeed, having put [it] on, we will not be found naked.

4. For indeed we who are in the tent groan, being burdened, because we do not wish to be unclothed but to be clothed upon, that the mortal may be swallowed up by the life.

5. And the [one] having prepared us for this very [thing] is God, who gave to us the guarantee of the Spirit.

6. Therefore being always confident and knowing that being at home in the body we are away from home from the Lord —

7. for by faith we walk, not by sight —

8. and we are confident and are well pleased rather to be away from home from the body and to be at home with the Lord.

9. Therefore also we are ambitious, whether at home or away from home, to be well-pleasing to him.

10. For we must all appear before the judgment seat of the Christos, that each one may receive the [things done] through the body, according to what he practiced, whether good or worthless.

11. Therefore knowing the fear of the Lord, we persuade people. And to God we have been made manifest; and I hope also in the consciences of you to have been made manifest.

12. We are not commending ourselves again to you but giving you an occasion of boast on behalf of us, that you may have [something] toward the [ones] boasting in face and not in heart.

13. For whether we were beside ourselves, [it was] for God; or whether we are of sound mind, [it is] for you.

14. For the love of the Christos constrains us, having judged this: that one died for all, therefore all died.

15. And he died for all, that the [ones] living might no longer live for themselves but for the [one] having died for them and having been raised.

16. So that we from now [on] know no one according to flesh. And if we have known Christos according to flesh, yet now we know [him] no longer.

17. So that if anyone [is] in Christos, [there is] a new creation; the old [things] have passed away; behold, new [things] have come.

18. And all [things are] from God, the [one] having reconciled us to himself through Christos and having given to us the ministry of the reconciliation,

19. as that God was in Christos reconciling [the] world to himself, not reckoning to them the trespasses of them, and having placed in us the word of the reconciliation.

20. Therefore on behalf of Christos we are ambassadors, as God is exhorting through us. We implore on behalf of Christos: be reconciled to God.

21. The [one] not knowing sin, on behalf of us he made sin, that we might become [the] righteousness of God in him.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "earthly house of the tent":** ἡ ἐπίγειος ἡμῶν οἰκία τοῦ σκήνους — a double metaphor: the body is both a house and a tent (temporary shelter). The tent imagery may echo the Feast of Tabernacles (sukkot).

2. **v.3 — "if indeed...not be found naked":** A textual difficulty. Sinaiticus reads εἴ γε ("if indeed") with ἐνδυσάμενοι ("having put on"). Some manuscripts read ἐκδυσάμενοι ("having put off"). The meaning: Paul hopes that at death, believers will not be disembodied but will be clothed with the heavenly body.

3. **v.10 — "judgment seat of the Christos":** βῆμα τοῦ Χριστοῦ — the βῆμα was the raised platform from which a Roman magistrate rendered judgment. In Corinth, the bema was a prominent public structure (cf. Acts 18:12-17).

4. **v.14 — "one died for all, therefore all died":** The logic: Christ's death was representative — when he died, all died in him. This is participation theology, not mere substitution.

5. **v.17 — "new creation":** καινὴ κτίσις — one of Paul's most important theological phrases. Not merely "new creature" but "new creation" — the eschatological renewal of all things has begun in Christ.

6. **v.21 — "he made sin":** ἁμαρτίαν ἐποίησεν — one of the most debated phrases in Paul. Christ was "made sin" — not merely a sin offering (though that may be included), but identified with sin itself, so that believers might be identified with God's righteousness.

— EMET, AI Translator
